mcqs.question: Which of the following is the MOST common form of secondary storage in traditional computing systems?
mcqs.explanation: Magnetic disks (hard disks) are the most common secondary storage devices in traditional computers. They provide non-volatile, high-capacity storage at relatively low cost compared to other storage technologies.

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of primary storage (RAM) compared to secondary storage?

options.text: Data is lost when power is turned off

mcqs.explanation: RAM is volatile memory - data is lost when power is removed. Secondary storage provides non-volatile storage, preserving data even when the system is powered off.

mcqs.question: In disk geometry, what is a cylinder?

options.text: A single circular track on one surface
The set of all tracks with the same radius across all surfaces
The distance from center to edge of the disk

mcqs.explanation: A cylinder consists of all tracks with the same radius on all platters. This allows the disk arm to access multiple surfaces without moving, as all heads can read their respective tracks simultaneously.

mcqs.question: Which component contributes the MOST to average disk access time?
mcqs.explanation: Seek time (the time to move the head to the correct track) typically dominates disk access time, ranging from 1-10ms. Rotational delay is usually half a rotation (2-5ms), and transfer time is much smaller for typical I/O operations.

mcqs.question: For a disk rotating at 10,000 RPM, what is the average rotational delay?
mcqs.explanation: At 10,000 RPM, one rotation takes 60/10000 = 6 ms. Average rotational delay is half a rotation = 3 ms.

mcqs.question: Which disk scheduling algorithm is also known as the elevator algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: The SCAN algorithm is called the elevator algorithm because it moves the head in one direction (like an elevator going up), servicing all requests, then reverses direction (goes down), servicing remaining requests.

mcqs.question: In the SSTF disk scheduling algorithm, what is the main drawback?

options.text: Starvation for requests far from current position
High overhead for maintaining queue order

mcqs.explanation: SSTF can cause starvation for requests that are far from the current head position, as closer requests are always prioritized. This can lead to delayed service for distant requests.

mcqs.question: What is low-level disk formatting used for?

options.text: Dividing the disk into sectors and writing headers/ECC

mcqs.explanation: Low-level (physical) formatting divides the disk surface into sectors, writes sector headers, error-correcting codes (ECC), and inter-sector gaps. This prepares the raw disk for use by creating the fundamental sector structure.

mcqs.question: What does the Master Boot Record (MBR) contain?

options.text: Boot strap loader and partition table

mcqs.explanation: The MBR contains the boot strap loader program (that starts the OS) and the partition table (which defines how the disk is divided into partitions). It is stored in the first sector of the disk.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a disk scheduling algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: LRU (Least Recently Used) is a cache replacement algorithm, not a disk scheduling algorithm. FCFS, SSTF, and LOOK are all disk scheduling algorithms used to order I/O requests.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of C-SCAN over SCAN?

options.text: More uniform wait times for all requests

mcqs.explanation: C-SCAN provides more uniform service times because it treats all tracks equally. Unlike SCAN where tracks near the ends get more frequent service, C-SCAN cycles through all tracks giving each request similar wait time.

mcqs.question: What is disk caching primarily used for?

options.text: Reducing access time for frequently accessed data

mcqs.explanation: Disk caching uses RAM to store frequently accessed disk data. Since RAM access is much faster than disk access, caching reduces the effective access time for repeated reads of the same data.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of disk partitioning?

options.text: To divide disk into independent logical sections
To format the disk for use

mcqs.explanation: Partitioning divides a physical disk into separate logical sections (partitions). Each partition can contain a different file system or operating system, allowing better organization and multiple uses of the same physical disk.

mcqs.question: Which type of latency is reduced by increasing disk rotational speed?
mcqs.explanation: Increasing rotational speed reduces rotational delay (rotational latency) because the disk completes a full rotation faster. For example, a 7200 RPM disk has half the rotational delay of a 3600 RPM disk.

mcqs.question: What is the typical sector size in modern hard disks?
mcqs.explanation: While traditional sectors were 512 bytes, modern hard disks typically use Advanced Format with 4096-byte (4 KB) sectors for improved efficiency and error correction.

mcqs.question: What is disk bandwidth?

options.text: The time to access a single sector
The total amount of data transferred per unit time
The number of tracks on the disk

mcqs.explanation: Disk bandwidth is the total amount of data that can be transferred from the disk to the computer in a given time period. It is measured in bytes per second and indicates the effective throughput of the disk system.

mcqs.question: Which component is NOT part of calculating average disk access time?
mcqs.explanation: Average disk access time = Average seek time + Average rotational delay + Transfer time. CPU processing time is not part of the physical disk access time calculation.

mcqs.question: What happens when data is written to a disk cache?

options.text: It is immediately written to disk (write-through)
It may be held in cache and written to disk later (write-back)
It is always lost on power failure

mcqs.explanation: In write-back caching, data is written to the cache first and written to the actual disk later. This improves write performance but risks data loss if power fails before data is written to disk. Write-through writes to both cache and disk immediately.

mcqs.question: What is the LOOK disk scheduling algorithm?
mcqs.key: LOOK is similar to SCAN but does not go to the end of the disk; it reverses direction at the last request. This avoids unnecessary head movement to the edge of the disk, improving efficiency.

options.text: Like SCAN but reverses at the last request, not the disk edge
Like FCFS but prioritizes larger requests
Like C-SCAN but uses circular queue
Like SSTF but checks all directions

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of Error-Correcting Code (ECC) in disk sectors?

options.text: To detect and correct data errors during read/write

mcqs.explanation: ECC is stored in each sector and allows the disk controller to detect and correct errors that may occur during data storage or retrieval. This ensures data integrity despite surface defects or interference.
